coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of cotton-silver-graphene quantum dots (cotton/Ag/GQDs) nanocomposite as a new antibacterial nanopad
coredata.dc:creator: Teymourinia, Hakimeh
Amiri, Omid
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: Appearance of antibiotic resistance in bacteria is a convoluted topic, particularly in treating pestiferous immunodeficiency correlated diseases. The main objective of the current research is to fabricate antibacterial pads by utilizing of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a linker, stabilizing, and reduction agent of in situ synthesized Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) on cotton pad. Five different antibacterial pads including cotton/Ag pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag pad, cotton/Ag/GQDs pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs pad, and cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag were fabricated and their antibacterial activities were compared to those of as-synthesized Ag/GQDs nanocomposites. The results indicate that cotton/GQDs/Ag pad shows a very promising minimum inhibitory concentration(MIC) of 0.09 and 0.01 against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Using GQDs as a linker (cotton/GQDs/Ag) and as a stabilizing agent (cotton/Ag/GQDs) significantly improves the antibacterial activity of Ag NPs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antibacterial
Nano pad
GQDs
Silver
Nanocomposite

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Ag NPs on pad

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of a) sample 1 and b) sample 8.
XRD pattern of different antibacterial pads: a) Config.1, b) Config.2, c) Config.3, d) Config.4 and e) Config.5.
EDX of sample 1, 8, cotton/GQDs/Ag pad, cotton/Ag/GQDs pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs pad and cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag.

The effect of different concentration of AgNO3: a) 0.17 M and b) 0.08 M, Ag/GQDs prepared with different reaction times: c) 7 min, d). 28 min, the effect of reaction temperature on the morphology of Ag/GQDs composite: e) 55 C and f) 70 C, g, h) SEM images of Ag/GQDs prepared in DI water.

HR-TEM images of Ag/GQDs prepared in DI water. Ag nanoparticle size range of 4–8 nm, and lattice spacing of 0.145 nm that matches with the (2 2 0) plane of silver. Ag nanoparticles surrounding by graphene sheet with lattice spacing of 0.214 nm that corresponds to the (1 0 0) lattice fringes of graphene.
PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs/Ag.
SEM images of a) cotton/GQDs and b) cotton/Ag pad, c) Config. 3, d) Config. 4, and e) Config. 5.
Shows optical density for growing E. coli and S. for a) sample 5 and b) cotton/GQDs/Ag pad.
Possible mechanism for degradation of bacterial by nano pad.
Experimental detail for synthesis of Ag/GQDs composite.
XRD detail for sample 1, 8, Config.1, Config.2, Config.Config.4 and Config.5

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N.G: No Growth and G: Growth.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antibacterial results for different antibacterial pads.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: N.G: No Growth and G: Growth.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The analogous bacterial performance of previous Ag and C based NMs reports with tis work.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NMs: Nanomaterials, MIC: Minimum inhibitory concentration.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of cotton-silver-graphene quantum dots (cotton/Ag/GQDs) nanocomposite as a new antibacterial nanopad

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P. O. Box. 87317-51167, I. R. Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Trita Nanomedicine Research Center (TNRC), Trita Third Millennium Pharmaceuticals, 45331-55681, Zanjan, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, International University of Erbil, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, College of Science, International University of Erbil, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Appearance of antibiotic resistance in bacteria is a convoluted topic, particularly in treating pestiferous immunodeficiency correlated diseases. The main objective of the current research is to fabricate antibacterial pads by utilizing of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as a linker, stabilizing, and reduction agent of in situ synthesized Ag nanoparticles (Ag NPs) on cotton pad. Five different antibacterial pads including cotton/Ag pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag pad, cotton/Ag/GQDs pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs pad, and cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag were fabricated and their antibacterial activities were compared to those of as-synthesized Ag/GQDs nanocomposites. The results indicate that cotton/GQDs/Ag pad shows a very promising minimum inhibitory concentration(MIC) of 0.09 and 0.01 against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Using GQDs as a linker (cotton/GQDs/Ag) and as a stabilizing agent (cotton/Ag/GQDs) significantly improves the antibacterial activity of Ag NPs.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, fatality scale of infectious patients has been enhanced owing to the emergence of drug-resistant types of bacteria that reject usage of antibiotics. Therefore, there is still a critical need for new antimicrobial agents. As it is estimated ∼80 000 cases have been inflected in the United States annually, with ∼11 000 deaths, Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus (MRSA) is one of specific concern which make this strain the most deadly antibiotic-resistant (Abdeen et al., 2018; Zong et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2019; Olleik et al., 2019). Moreover, several other Gram-positive and Gram-negative kinds of pathogens like E. coli that can arouse diseases like dysentery and diarrhea have become resistant at a worrying level (Chanter et al., 1986; Celebioglu et al., 2019). That’s why antibacterial materials have been of notable interest in aiming pathogenic kinds of bacteria in everyday human life as well as preserving health of human (Li et al., 2015).

Therefore, special attention must be dedicated not only to the evolution of new class of antibiotics but also researchers need to work on new types of antibacterial surfaces that will reduce or suppress the evolvement of bacterial biofilms on all available surfaces. Bacteria can form biofilms anywhere: in food, water or on any other surfaces.

Owing to its unique attributes like a great surface area and very proper performance against an extensive kinds of pathogens, Ag NPs has been wildly used as an antibacterial agent (Hernández-Arteaga, Nava et al., 2017; Alsammarraie et al., 2018; Saravanan et al., 2018). However, previous reports indicate that Ag NPs could result in health and ecological problems. They specified that the poisonousness of nanostructured Ag may be primarily caused by silver ions leaked from nanostructured silver, and the poisonousness largely relies upon the quantity of the leaked ions (Parham et al., 2017; Cáceres-Vélez et al., 2018; Falconer et al., 2018; Falconer and Grainger 2018). These issues limited the application of Ag NPs in other industries such as the cotton textile industry. The possible solution could be improved by bonding Ag NPs to the cotton fiber surface. Until now, a great number of approaches have been stated to improve bond between Ag NPs and the surface of the cotton fiber (He et al., 2018; Karim et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018).

Polymeric binders have been employed by most of these methods, because different functional groups can be combined in one polymer chain and link with cotton fabric to immobilize the Ag NPs (Li et al., 2018).

In this work, we have used green synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) as both the reducing and immobilizing agents to directly synthesize Ag nanoparticles (NPs) on the cotton pad, while previous reports commonly used Ag colloid and linked Ag NPs by polymeric binders (Shankar and Rhim 2017; Xu et al., 2017). Our methods have some advantages such as more effective immobilization and higher antibacterial activity. Direct synthesis of Ag NPs on cotton leads to improved immobilization of Ag NPs because Ag+ cation is first absorbed by COH group on the cotton structures, then is reduced to the Ag NPs by GQDs. Besides, GQDs tie the Ag NPs to the surface of cotton. GQDs have a variety of functional groups such as CO, COOH, etc. that could link Ag NPs and cotton surface. Increasing antibacterial activity is another advantage that was achieved because GQDs itself shows the antibacterial effect, collaboration effects, and increase the antibacterial activity of Ag NPs (Liu et al., 2018; Marković et al., 2018). According to the previous reports, GQDs show very good antibacterial and anticancer activity towards different bacteria and cancer cells under light irradiation (Marković et al., 2017; Marković et al., 2018; Stanković, Bodik et al., 2018; Marković et al., 2019). Different parameters such as the sequence of GQDs and Ag were studied. We also fabricated Ag/GQDs nanocomposite and compared its antibacterial activity with Ag NPs on a pad. Antibacterial results show that Ag/GQDs on a pad show much more antibacterial activities compared to Ag/GQDs nanocomposite. Ag NPs on a pad suggests minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) against S. aureus and E Coli. This is minimum concentration of antibacterial nanostructures, where no bacterial growth was observed.
The MIC of 0.01 and 0.09 was accomplished for Ag NPs on a pad, while it was 0.06 and also 0.4 for Ag/GQD against E Coli as well as S. aureus, correspondingly.

We used our previous method to prepare graphene quantum dots (Teymourinia, Salavati-Niasari et al. 2018, 2019). Therefore, 250 mg of powdered corn has been added into 25 cc of H2O, followed by stirring for 0.5 h. Ultimately, we heated the admixture at 180 °C for 48 h into a autoclave.

The fabricated GQDs were used in role of the reducer and also a latter element of the target composite to prepare Ag/GQDs nanocomposite. We added 3 cc of the fabricated GQDs admixture to the 5 cc of AgNO3 solution and afterwards stirred it within 14 min. Details of experiment is presented in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Ag NPs on pad
ce:section.ce:para: In this section different antibacterial pads were prepared to achieve the highest antibacterial activity. As the first configuration, Ag Pad was prepared by dropping AgNO3 solution on the cotton pad. After 24–48 h, Ag NPs appeared on the cotton surface (Config. 1). This happens because OH-group in the cotton could reduce Ag+ ion to Ag NPs. The second configuration was achieved by following the sequence of cotton/GQDs/Ag (Config. 2). GQDs solution was dropped on cotton followed by dropping AgNO3 solution and heating at 50 C. Here, GQDs act as both reducing agent and linker. Config. 3 was achieved by dropping AgNO3 solution on cotton pad followed by dropping GQDs solution. Pad/GQDs/Ag/GQDs and Pad/Ag/GQDs/Ag show the Configs. 4 and 5, respectively.

We followed the instructions offered with CLSI to study the activity of antibacterial Ag/GQDs composite and related pads. The minimum inhibitory concentrations (MIC) of as-prepared composite and related pads were measured for E. coli as well as S. aureus; incubated during 1 day at 37 °C and followed the protocol formerly reported by Teymournia et al. (Teymourinia, Salavati-Niasari et al., 2019; Teymourinia, Salavati-Niasari et al., 2019).

Crystalline phase of the as-prepared nanostructures have been identified with the help of a diffractometer of Philips Company with Nifiltered Cu Ka radiation. Morphology and dimension of the as-prepared nanostructures have been checked with FESEM (MIRA3 FEGSEM) and TEM (JEM-2100). The light absorbance attribute of the as-prepared nanostructures has been checked with a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-2550, Japan).

In the present study, we first prepare Ag/GQDs nanocomposite. We examine different synthesis variables like reaction temperature and AgNO3 quantity on the shape and antibacterial performance of Ag/GQDs nanocomposite. Then, we fabricate Config. 1–5 as antibacterial pads to improve the antibacterial activity of Ag. As-prepared Ag pad (Config. 2) shows 6 times more antibacterial activities compared to the Ag/GQDs nanocomposite.

XRD pattern of different Ag/GQDs nanocomposites depict in Fig. 1 . Fig. 2 shows the XRD patterns for different configuration of antibacterial pads. XRD pattern for configuration 1 shows the Ag ions reduced by cotton (Fig. 2a). Furthermore, the broad and amorphous-like pattern is related to the cotton pad. XRD pattern of Config. 2 shows that GQDs covered the Ag nanoparticles, leading to decreases in the intensity of peaks related to the Ag (Fig. 2b). Fig. 2c–e shows the XRD pattern of Config. 3–5, respectively. According to these results, Config. 4 shows the lowest Ag intensity peak because of using two layers of GQDs. It should be noted that the average crystallite size of as synthesized nanostructures obtained by the Scherrer equation (L = 0.89λ/β cosθ), whereas, β is the full width at half maximum (FWHM) intensity of the peak in Radian (Amiri, Akbari et al., 2018; Amiri, Pardakhti et al., 2018). Table 2 shows the details for FWHM and crystal size of different samples. According to the Table 2, Config. 5 shows the lowest crystal size.

Fig. 3 shows the EDX of sample 1, 8, cotton/GQDs/Ag pad, cotton/Ag/GQDs pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs pad, and cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag. EDX analysis was used as strong evidence to approve the presence of Ag and C. The EDX outcomes approved the existence of carbon corresponding to GQDs and silver.

The effect of the quantity of AgNO3, solvent kind, reaction temperature, and also time upon the shape of Ag/GQDs was studied. Fig. 4 a and b shows the effect of varied concentrations of AgNO3 on the formation of spherical particles when AgNO3 with the concentration of 0.17 M was used (Fig. 4a). Fig. 4 a illustrates particles stuck together. By diluting the concentration of Ag precursor to 0.08 M, separate spherical particles were achieved (Fig. 4b). The prepared Ag/GQDs and different reaction times are denoted in Fig. 4 c and d. Here, the reaction time reflects the time that GQDs were added to the AgNO3 solution. When the reaction time was 7 min, separated particles were achieved (Fig. 4c). The smaller particles that stuck together were synthesized by increasing the reaction time to 14 min (Fig. 4a). More increase in reaction time (28 min), results in the production of large particles (Fig. 4c). Three different temperatures (25 C, 55 C, and 70 C) were tested to evaluate the effect of reaction temperature. According to Fig. 4a, e and f, increasing the temperature from 25 to 55 C leads to an increase in the size of the prepared nanomaterials, but more separated particles are obtained. Smaller and non-uniform particles obtained were by changing temperature to 70 C. To study the effect of the solvent, synthesis was done in DI water (Fig. 4g and h). As SEM images show, sponge-like nanostructures were achieved when Ag+ was reduced by GQDs in the aqueous environment.

To provide data related to the shape, dimension, and crystalline nature of composite specimen, HRTEM microscopy was conducted. Fig. 5 a and b illustrate Ag-GQDs composite HR-TEM images. Ag and GQDs are shown in Fig. 5 a. Dark dots represent Ag NPs and bright sheets represent GQDs. The HR-TEM of Ag/GQDs composite illustrate Ag nanoparticle size range of 4–8 nm, and also lattice spacing (0.145 nm) which matches with (2 2 0) planes of silver. As illustrated in Fig. 5, Ag NPs surrounded by graphene sheet with lattice spacing (0.214 nm) correspond to (1 0 0) lattice fringes in graphene. Fig. 6 shows the PL spectra of GQDs and GQDs/Ag. GQDs shows the emission peak at 410 nm. By adding Ag emission peak shifted to 440 nm.

Fig. 7 shows data for a) cotton/GQDs and also b) cotton/GQDs/Ag pad. As the results show, GQDs cover the cotton pad (Fig. 7 a). In Fig. 7 b, we have used GQDs as a linker. It could be observed that Ag NPs covered the GQDs. SEM images of cotton/Ag/GQDs, cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag, and cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs are shown in Fig. 7c–e. In Fig. 7 c, GQDs covered the Ag NPs. We observed an increase in the amount of Ag NPs for Ag/GQDs/Ag pad (Fig. 7 d). In Fig. 7 e, we have used GQDs as a linker and as a stabilizing agent. It may well be observed that GQDs covered the nanostructured silver.

The antibacterial activity for different silver/GQDs composite, cotton/Ag pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag pad, cotton/Ag/GQDs pad, cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs pad, and cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag were evaluated against E. coli and S. aureus. Fig. 8 schematically describes suggested arrangement of different configurations. As seen in Fig. 8, Ag NPs are directly bonded to the cotton in Config.1 (Fig. 8a). We have suggested Config. 2 (Fig. 8b) to improve the photocatalytic activity of Ag and its bonding to the cotton. In this regard, GQDs could act as linker because of its proper interaction with cotton and Ag NPs. As seen in Fig. 8c, Config. 3 was suggested to figure out the effect of GQDs in role of a stabilizing agent for fix nanostructured silver upon cotton. In Config. 4, GQDS was first coated on cotton to link cotton and Ag NPs (GQDs1 in the schematic). Afterwards, GQDs covered the Ag layer to maximize Ag immobilizing (Fig. 8d, GQDs2 in the schematic). In Config. 5, GQDs act as a linker and as a stabilizing agent at the same time (Fig. 8e). As schematically observed, GQDs act as a stabilizing agent for Ag1 and as a linker for Ag2.

Table 3 shows the MIC values of samples 1–8 compared to E. coli and S. aureus. We examined three concentrations for each nanomaterial. For example, different concentrations of sample 1 including 0.02, 0.05, and also 0.08 for E. coli as well as 0.25, 0.35, and 0.40 for S. aureus have been tried, correspondingly. No development was observed for E. coli and also S. aureus when 0.11 and 0.74 were used, correspondingly. The greatest antibacterial activity was accomplished for sample 5 where its MIC was 0.06 and also 0.40 for E. coli as well as S. aureus, correspondingly. The MIC values of pad/GQDs and Config.1–5 are presented in Table 4 . According to Table 4, pad/GQDs show the lowest antibacterial activity (higher quantity is needed to suspend the growth of E. coli as well as S. aureus). This illustrated that GQDs did not show any great antibacterial activity. Config. 2 (pad/GQDs/Ag) demonstrate the greatest antibacterial performance against both kinds of bacteria, namely E. coli and also S. aureus. It is very promising that MIC was 0.01 and 0.09 in the case of E. coli and S. aureus, correspondingly. With comparing MIC in Configs.1 and 2, we figure out that when GQDs act as a linker, it leads to improving the antibacterial activity of Ag NPs (MIC for Config. 1: 0.09 and also 0.40 in the case of E. coli and S. aureus). By using GQDs as stabilizing agents to fix Ag NPs (Config. 3), MIC increased to 0.02 and also 0.2 in the case of E. coli and S. aureus (antibacterial activity decreased compared to the Config. 2). However, it still shows a promising MIC. Using two layers of GQDs as a linker and as a stabling agent (Config. 4) and using GQDs as a linker and as a stabling agent at the same time (Config. 5), they decrease the antibacterial activity of nanostructured silver. Table 5 . Presents comparison of the outcomes from recent antimicrobial studies of the various Silver and Carbon based nanomaterials (Ag and C based NMs) with our results (Zhu et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016; Zeng et al., 2016; Simbine et al., 2019). Optical density in the case of culturing E. coli and also S. aureus for sample 5 and Config. 2 are illustrated in Fig. 9 . Decreasing the optical density of bacterial culture in the case of E. coli and also S. aureus confirms that bacterial growth rate is reduced over time. Fig. 10 describes possible mechanism for destroying bacteria by our prepared pads. Previous reports show reactive oxidative species (ROS) play a main role in the GQDs/Ag NPs antibacterial function. To explain the mechanism of ROS production, the generation of electron-hole pairs is the first step. Then, reduction of O2 to superoxide radicals (•O2 −) has been done in the conduction band of GQDs and Ag NPs. Further, oxidation of •O2 − by photogenerated holes from the valence band of GQDs and Ag NPs leads to the formation of singlet oxygen (1O2). Also, hydroxyl radicals (•OH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and protonated superoxide radical (•HO2) have been produced by the reaction of holes in the valence band of GQDs and Ag NPs with H2O or OH− that adsorbed on the surface of the nanomaterials (Simões et al., 2008). Moreover, the reaction of H2O2 with •HO leads to form a superoxide radical (•HO2) (Fuentes et al., 1998). According to reports, all types of these ROS (•O2 −, •OH, 1O2, •HO2, and H2O2) contribute to the major oxidative stress in biological systems. Including, organic biomolecules, such as carbohydrates, nucleic acids, lipids, proteins, DNA, and amino acids successfully have been damage strongly and nonselective by ·OH. Also, 1O2 can damage the treated tissues irreversibly, results in the degradation of biomembrane (Chen et al., 2017; Travlou et al., 2018; Xin et al., 2019). Eventually, all reactive oxygen species (ROS), that are produced from the reaction of generated electron-hole pairs with H2O, OH−, and O2 results in the decomposition of bacteria (Chen et al., 2017; Teymourinia, Salavati-Niasari et al., 2019).

In summary, we applied GQDs to prepare antibacterial pads based on Ag NPs. To achieve this goal, GQDs was used as a linker and as a stabilizing agent. Based on these properties, we suggested 5 different configurations including cotton/Ag pad (Config. 1), cotton/GQDs/Ag pad (Config. 2), cotton/Ag/GQDs pad (Config. 3), cotton/GQDs/Ag/GQDs pad (Config. 4), and cotton/Ag/GQDs/Ag (Config. 5). We also prepared Ag/GQDs nanocomposites under different synthesis conditions to figure the effect of immobilizing Ag on cotton. According to the antibacterial results, sample 5 shows the highest antibacterial activity among Ag/GQDs nanocomposite with MIC of 0.06 and also 0.40 in the case of E. coli as well as S. aureus, correspondingly. We figured out that Configs. 1 and 2 show better antibacterial performance compared to the Ag/GQDs nanocomposite (sample 5) and other Configs. The very promising MIC of 0.01 and 0.09 was achieved for Config. 2 against E. coli as well as S. aureus, respectively. We can conclude that utilizing GQDs as a linker (Config. 2) and as a stabilizing agent (Config. 3) significantly improves the antibacterial activity of Ag NPs. Moreover, the results have shown when GQDs functions as linker and stabilizing agent at the same time (Configs. 4 and 5), however, prevents agglomeration of silver nanoparticles but also makes weaken conductivity of the electron which cause reduced antibacterial properties.

Hakimeh Teymourinia: Investigation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - original draft, Software. Omid Amiri: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Software. Masoud Salavati-Niasari: Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Resources, Data Curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837), contract (98002158) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/12).
